refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) remains at or below the customer-defined E_target throughout the evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across the range of supported worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 is a key performance indicator (KPI) requirement that must be decomposed into measurable, controllable functional elements. The refined statement clarifies that energy efficiency is a system-level property emerging from multiple subsystems (propulsion, hydraulics, teleoperation overhead, idle management, load optimization). It establishes the measurement boundary (energy_per_ton), the acceptance criterion (≤ E_target), and the evaluation context (nominal conditions, worksite scenarios). High safety criticality tailoring requires that energy efficiency measures do not degrade safety-critical functions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Establish a continuous energy monitoring and reporting pipeline: (1) Instrument Vehicle with energy sensors (fuel/battery consumption, electrical draw); (2) Integrate ton_moved measurement from load cells or task completion logs; (3) Aggregate energy_per_ton metric in real-time via Telemetry interface; (4) Compare against E_target baseline; (5) Generate efficiency reports for Site Manager and Maintenance stakeholders. Verification occurs over the agreed evaluation period with statistical confidence.
sub_frs: FR-EE-1: The Vehicle shall minimize energy consumption per unit work output through optimized propulsion, hydraulic, and drivetrain efficiency.
FR-EE-2: The Teleoperation subsystem shall minimize energy overhead (network, compute, latency compensation) relative to direct operation.
FR-EE-3: The NGHE solution shall continuously monitor and report energy consumption and work output (ton_moved) to enable real-time and post-hoc efficiency assessment.
FR-EE-4: The Safety Services subsystem shall ensure that energy efficiency measures do not compromise safety-critical functions or introduce new hazards.

candidate_dps: DP-EE-A: Implement a multi-layer energy optimization strategy: (1) Vehicle-level: advanced powertrain efficiency (hybrid/electric propulsion, regenerative braking, load-adaptive hydraulics); (2) Teleoperation-level: latency-optimized control protocols, edge compute for local autonomy; (3) System-level: real-time energy monitoring, predictive load balancing, operator coaching on efficient techniques.

DP-EE-B: Implement a passive energy efficiency design: specify high-efficiency components (motors, pumps, transmissions) at procurement; rely on operator training and worksite best practices; no active monitoring or feedback.

DP-EE-C: Implement energy efficiency via software-only optimization: real-time telemetry collection, machine learning-based predictive control, and dynamic task scheduling without hardware modifications.

DP-EE-D: Implement a hybrid approach: Vehicle hardware efficiency improvements (DP-EE-A layer 1) + centralized energy monitoring and reporting (DP-EE-A layer 3) + operator feedback loop, deferring advanced teleoperation optimization to a future phase.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-EE-D: Implement a hybrid, phased energy efficiency strategy combining: (1) Vehicle-level propulsion and hydraulic efficiency improvements (high-efficiency motors, pumps, load-sensing hydraulics, idle shutdown); (2) System-level continuous energy monitoring via instrumented sensors on Vehicle, aggregation through Telemetry interface, and real-time reporting to Site Manager and Maintenance dashboards; (3) Operator feedback mechanisms (efficiency alerts, coaching) integrated into Teleoperation interface; (4) Safety Services validation that efficiency measures do not degrade safety-critical functions. Defer advanced teleoperation latency optimization and predictive autonomy to Phase 2.

potential_sources: Energy monitoring (FR-EE-3) is decoupled from propulsion efficiency (FR-EE-1) via the Telemetry interface; monitoring does not mandate specific propulsion architecture.
Teleoperation overhead (FR-EE-2) is addressed via operator feedback and coaching, not by modifying core teleoperation protocols in Phase 1; this defers coupling risk.
Safety validation (FR-EE-4) is a gate function, not a design parameter; Safety Services will verify non-interference via SafetyAlert interface.
DP-EE-D avoids tight coupling between Vehicle hardware, Teleoperation, and Safety by using well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert) and phased rollout.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling risk is moderate: energy efficiency improvements in Vehicle (e.g., load-sensing hydraulics) may interact with operator control feel and safety margins. Mitigation: Safety Services must validate control responsiveness and stability under all efficiency modes. Teleoperation overhead is deferred, reducing Phase 1 coupling.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Vehicle hardware can be modified to support high-efficiency components (motors, pumps, load-sensing systems) within cost and schedule constraints.
ASSUMPTION: Telemetry infrastructure (sensors, network, aggregation) can be deployed and maintained reliably across all worksite scenarios.
ASSUMPTION: Operator training and feedback mechanisms will be adopted by Site Manager and Equipment Operator stakeholders.
RISK: If Vehicle hardware modifications are not feasible or are delayed, energy efficiency gains will be limited to monitoring and behavioral feedback (lower impact).
RISK: If Telemetry infrastructure is unreliable or has high latency, real-time energy reporting and operator feedback will be degraded.
RISK: If operators do not adopt efficiency coaching, behavioral improvements will not materialize; requires change management and incentive alignment.
RISK: Load-sensing hydraulics and other efficiency measures may introduce new failure modes or maintenance complexity; Maintenance/Quality must validate.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify with stakeholders: (1) Vehicle hardware modification scope and constraints (cost, schedule, compatibility with existing platforms); (2) Telemetry infrastructure availability and reliability SLAs; (3) Operator training and incentive model for efficiency adoption. If hardware modifications are severely constrained, pivot to DP-EE-C (software-only) or DP-EE-B (passive) with adjusted E_target expectations.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system integration boundary that orchestrates energy efficiency across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. It owns the energy monitoring and reporting pipeline, the operator feedback loop, and the integration of efficiency measures with safety validation. The NGHE Solution module is responsible for: (1) defining and maintaining the energy_per_ton KPI; (2) aggregating energy and work output data from Vehicle via Telemetry; (3) distributing efficiency targets and feedback to Vehicle and Teleoperation; (4) coordinating with Safety Services to ensure efficiency measures do not compromise safety; (5) reporting efficiency metrics to Site Manager and Maintenance stakeholders.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives energy consumption (fuel/battery draw, electrical power) and work output (ton_moved, task completion) from Vehicle via Telemetry. NGHE Solution sends efficiency targets, mode commands, and feedback to Vehicle via Telemetry. Telemetry is the primary data channel for energy monitoring and real-time reporting.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution sends efficiency mode changes and control parameter updates to Safety Services via SafetyAlert for validation. Safety Services returns safety clearance or constraints (e.g., 'load-sensing hydraulics approved for normal operations, not emergency descent'). SafetyAlert ensures efficiency measures do not degrade safety-critical functions.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives task definitions (load, distance, terrain, time window) from Site Manager via Tasking. Task context informs energy efficiency optimization (e.g., predictive load balancing, route optimization). Tasking enables worksite scenario modeling for efficiency evaluation.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution uses Network Link to communicate with Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, and external dashboards (Site Manager, Maintenance). Network Link carries Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Tasking payloads. Network reliability and latency directly impact real-time energy reporting and operator feedback.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level owner of the energy efficiency KPI and the integration of efficiency measures across subsystems. Energy monitoring, reporting, and feedback orchestration are system-level concerns. Containment is very high. Minimal diffusion: only the Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces extend to Vehicle and Safety Services, which is appropriate for subsystem integration.

Vehicle owns propulsion and hydraulic efficiency improvements (FR-EE-1), which are core to energy_per_ton reduction. However, Vehicle does not own the system-level energy monitoring, reporting, or operator feedback (FR-EE-3, FR-EE-4). If energy monitoring were fully contained in Vehicle, it would create diffusion: Vehicle would need to expose energy data, efficiency metrics, and feedback mechanisms to multiple external consumers (Site Manager, Maintenance, Teleoperation). Assigning monitoring to NGHE Solution (via Telemetry interface) reduces diffusion and centralizes KPI ownership.

Teleoperation is responsible for operator feedback and coaching (part of FR-EE-2), but does not own propulsion efficiency (Vehicle), energy monitoring (NGHE Solution), or safety validation (Safety Services). Assigning the entire energy efficiency KPI to Teleoperation would create high diffusion: Teleoperation would need to coordinate with Vehicle, Safety Services, and external dashboards. Teleoperation is a consumer of efficiency feedback, not the owner.

Safety Services owns the validation that efficiency measures do not compromise safety (FR-EE-4), but does not own propulsion efficiency, energy monitoring, or operator feedback. Assigning the entire KPI to Safety Services would create diffusion: Safety Services would need to drive efficiency improvements in Vehicle and Teleoperation, which is not its primary role. Safety Services is a gate/validator, not the owner of efficiency optimization.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the optimal owner of CR-NGHE-003 (Energy efficiency KPI) because: (1) it is the system-level integration point that can aggregate energy and work output data from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; (2) it owns the energy_per_ton metric definition, measurement, and reporting; (3) it can coordinate efficiency targets and feedback across subsystems without creating diffusion; (4) it can interface with external stakeholders (Site Manager, Maintenance) via well-defined Telemetry and SafetyAlert channels. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are subsystem contributors to efficiency, but not owners of the KPI.
containment_risks: RISK: If NGHE Solution does not have sufficient authority or control over Vehicle hardware decisions, energy efficiency improvements may be delayed or compromised. Mitigation: Establish clear ownership of Vehicle efficiency improvements (propulsion, hydraulics) and escalation paths for conflicts.

RISK: If Telemetry infrastructure is not reliable or has high latency, real-time energy reporting and operator feedback will be degraded, undermining the effectiveness of NGHE Solution's monitoring and feedback mechanisms. Mitigation: Define Telemetry SLAs (latency, availability, data accuracy) and validate against worksite conditions.

RISK: If Safety Services validation is slow or creates bottlenecks, efficiency improvements may be delayed. Mitigation: Establish clear safety validation criteria and fast-track approval for low-risk efficiency measures (e.g., idle shutdown).

RISK: If operator feedback mechanisms are not integrated into Teleoperation UI/UX, adoption will be low. Mitigation: Involve Teleoperation and Equipment Operator stakeholders in feedback design and testing.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm with System Engineer and Project Manager: (1) NGHE Solution has authority to define and enforce energy efficiency targets across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; (2) Telemetry infrastructure can support real-time energy monitoring with <5s latency and >99% availability; (3) Safety Services can validate efficiency measures within 2-week turnaround; (4) Teleoperation UI/UX can accommodate efficiency feedback without degrading operator situational awareness. If any of these conditions cannot be met, escalate to architecture review and consider alternative IM assignments or phased rollout.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-EE-D (hybrid, phased energy efficiency strategy) directly addresses CR-NGHE-003 (Energy efficiency KPI) by combining Vehicle-level propulsion efficiency, system-level energy monitoring and reporting, operator feedback, and safety validation. The DP is traceable to the refined FR and its sub-FRs (FR-EE-1 through FR-EE-4). The DP is feasible within Phase 1 scope (hardware improvements + monitoring + feedback) and defers higher-risk items (advanced teleoperation optimization) to Phase 2. The DP includes explicit safety validation (FR-EE-4), addressing the high safety criticality tailoring factor. No unresolved contradictions between FR and DP.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK (Low): Energy monitoring and reporting are centralized in NGHE Solution, reducing diffusion compared to distributing monitoring across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. However, NGHE Solution must interface with all three subsystems via Telemetry and SafetyAlert, creating a hub-and-spoke dependency. Mitigation: Ensure Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces are well-defined, versioned, and stable.

DIFFUSION RISK (Low): Operator feedback mechanisms are distributed between NGHE Solution (feedback generation) and Teleoperation (feedback presentation). This is acceptable because Teleoperation owns the operator interface; NGHE Solution provides the data. Mitigation: Define a clear feedback data contract between NGHE Solution and Teleoperation.

DIFFUSION RISK (Medium): Vehicle efficiency improvements (propulsion, hydraulics) are owned by Vehicle module, but NGHE Solution must monitor and report on their effectiveness. This creates a dependency: if Vehicle efficiency improvements are delayed or incomplete, NGHE Solution's energy_per_ton metric will not meet E_target. Mitigation: Establish clear Vehicle efficiency improvement roadmap and milestones; track progress via Telemetry.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for the energy efficiency KPI because it owns the system-level integration, measurement, and reporting. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are subsystem contributors with clear, bounded responsibilities. The Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, and Network Link interfaces provide clean separation of concerns. No significant containment violations detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: CR-NGHE-003 is verifiable via continuous energy monitoring and reporting: (1) Instrument Vehicle with energy sensors (fuel/battery consumption, electrical draw); (2) Integrate ton_moved measurement from load cells or task completion logs; (3) Aggregate energy_per_ton metric in real-time via Telemetry interface; (4) Compare against E_target baseline; (5) Generate efficiency reports for Site Manager and Maintenance stakeholders. Verification occurs over the agreed evaluation period with statistical confidence. NGHE Solution module is responsible for steps 3-5; Vehicle is responsible for steps 1-2. Verification is feasible if Telemetry infrastructure is reliable and energy sensors are accurate.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CR-NGHE-003 is allocated to NGHE Solution with clear sub-allocations to Vehicle (FR-EE-1: propulsion efficiency), Teleoperation (FR-EE-2: teleoperation overhead), NGHE Solution (FR-EE-3: energy monitoring and reporting), and Safety Services (FR-EE-4: safety validation). Each sub-FR is traceable to a specific module and has clear ownership. No ambiguity in allocation.

recommended_revisions: Establish Telemetry SLAs: latency <5s, availability >99%, energy sensor accuracy ±5%. Validate against worksite network conditions (cellular, WiFi, satellite).
Define energy_per_ton baseline and E_target with Site Manager and Maintenance stakeholders. Establish evaluation period (e.g., 90 days) and statistical confidence level (e.g., 95%).
Clarify Vehicle hardware modification scope: which propulsion and hydraulic efficiency improvements are in scope for Phase 1? What is the cost and schedule impact?
Establish Safety Services validation criteria for efficiency measures. Define fast-track approval process for low-risk measures (e.g., idle shutdown).
Involve Teleoperation and Equipment Operator stakeholders in operator feedback design. Validate that feedback does not degrade situational awareness or safety.
Document assumptions and risks in the design record: hardware feasibility, Telemetry reliability, operator adoption, maintenance complexity.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-003 (Energy efficiency KPI) is a system-level requirement that has been successfully refined into four sub-FRs (FR-EE-1 through FR-EE-4), each with clear ownership and interfaces. The selected DP (DP-EE-D: hybrid, phased strategy) is feasible and addresses the high safety criticality tailoring factor. The assigned IM (NGHE Solution) is the appropriate system-level owner with clear interfaces to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with manageable risks. The next step is to decompose each sub-FR to the next level of detail, establishing the detailed design parameters and implementation modules for Vehicle efficiency (FR-EE-1), Teleoperation overhead (FR-EE-2), energy monitoring (FR-EE-3), and safety validation (FR-EE-4). This will enable detailed design, component selection, and verification planning.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-EE-1: The Vehicle shall minimize energy consumption per unit work output through optimized propulsion, hydraulic, and drivetrain efficiency.
FR-EE-2: The Teleoperation subsystem shall minimize energy overhead (network, compute, latency compensation) relative to direct operation.
FR-EE-3: The NGHE solution shall continuously monitor and report energy consumption and work output (ton_moved) to enable real-time and post-hoc efficiency assessment.
FR-EE-4: The Safety Services subsystem shall ensure that energy efficiency measures do not compromise safety-critical functions or introduce new hazards.

assumptions: Vehicle hardware can be modified to support high-efficiency components (motors, pumps, load-sensing systems) within cost and schedule constraints.
Telemetry infrastructure (sensors, network, aggregation) can be deployed and maintained reliably across all worksite scenarios with <5s latency and >99% availability.
Operator training and feedback mechanisms will be adopted by Site Manager and Equipment Operator stakeholders.
Safety Services can validate efficiency measures within 2-week turnaround without creating bottlenecks.
Energy sensors on Vehicle can achieve ±5% accuracy in fuel/battery consumption and electrical draw measurement.
Ton_moved measurement can be derived reliably from load cells, task completion logs, or other worksite data sources.
E_target is defined by customer and is achievable with Phase 1 efficiency improvements (hardware + monitoring + feedback).
Evaluation period is sufficient (e.g., 90 days) to establish statistical confidence in energy_per_ton metric.
open_risks: Vehicle hardware modifications may be delayed or constrained by cost, schedule, or compatibility with existing platforms. Impact: energy efficiency gains limited to monitoring and behavioral feedback (lower impact). Mitigation: Establish clear Vehicle efficiency improvement roadmap and milestones.

Telemetry infrastructure may be unreliable or have high latency in certain worksite scenarios (e.g., remote locations, poor cellular coverage). Impact: real-time energy reporting and operator feedback degraded. Mitigation: Define Telemetry SLAs and validate against worksite conditions.

Operators may not adopt efficiency coaching or feedback mechanisms. Impact: behavioral improvements will not materialize. Mitigation: Establish operator training and incentive model for efficiency adoption.

Load-sensing hydraulics and other efficiency measures may introduce new failure modes or maintenance complexity. Impact: maintenance costs and downtime may increase. Mitigation: Maintenance/Quality must validate and establish maintenance procedures.

Safety Services validation may create bottlenecks or conflicts with efficiency improvements. Impact: efficiency improvements delayed. Mitigation: Establish clear safety validation criteria and fast-track approval for low-risk measures.

Energy_per_ton metric may not be achievable with Phase 1 improvements alone. Impact: E_target not met. Mitigation: Establish realistic E_target with customer; plan Phase 2 improvements (advanced teleoperation, predictive autonomy).
evidence_needed: Vehicle hardware modification feasibility study: cost, schedule, compatibility analysis for high-efficiency motors, pumps, load-sensing hydraulics.
Telemetry infrastructure assessment: network coverage, latency, availability, sensor accuracy validation across representative worksite scenarios.
Energy sensor specification and calibration: fuel/battery consumption, electrical draw measurement accuracy and reliability.
Ton_moved measurement methodology: load cell specifications, task completion log integration, data quality validation.
E_target definition and baseline: customer-defined target, current baseline energy_per_ton, gap analysis, improvement roadmap.
Safety validation criteria: list of efficiency measures, safety impact assessment, validation procedures, approval process.
Operator feedback design: mockups, usability testing, integration with Teleoperation UI/UX, training plan.
Maintenance impact assessment: failure mode analysis, maintenance procedures, spare parts, training for Maintenance/Quality stakeholders.
Phase 1 vs. Phase 2 roadmap: clear delineation of in-scope and out-of-scope efficiency improvements, schedule, dependencies.
